title: Nail Polish on Wool
stainChemistry: Nail polish is a nitrocellulose film with pigments in ester solvents (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate) plus small amounts of acetone. On wool, the polish film bonds to the scaled cuticle of each fiber as the solvent evaporates. Wool is a protein fiber (keratin) with a scaled surface that swells and felts when exposed to water + agitation. Acetone does not dissolve wool but dehydrates the keratin scales, causing permanent hand loss and crazing of the fiber surface. Isopropyl alcohol strips dyes and raises the scales. Heat and friction during attempted removal felt the fiber irreversibly. The solvents that reverse the polish film damage the wool substrate.
whyThisWorks: Amyl acetate applied by a professional spotter is the only fiber-safe route for polish on wool. Amyl acetate softens nitrocellulose without attacking protein fibers, and a trained spotter works cold with no agitation to prevent felting, uses NSD for pigment lift, and neutralizes with dilute acetic acid at pH ~5 to preserve keratin. This requires professional chemistry and fiber control wool cannot tolerate at home. Household 'non-acetone' removers still contain uncontrolled ester concentrations that damage wool.
defaultAssumption: Assume the customer does NOT have amyl acetate, professional spotting experience, or the temperature control needed to avoid felting wool. Default recommendation is professional dry-clean referral. Do not offer consumer-tier DIY chemical suggestions on this card.

spottingProtocol.instruction: This combination is a professional-only case. Take the garment to a dry cleaner with wool-spotting experience and tell them: (1) the stain is nail polish, (2) the fiber is wool, (3) you have NOT applied any solvent, remover, or water at home, (4) you have NOT rubbed the spot. Do not use acetone, do not use 'non-acetone' polish remover, do not use rubbing alcohol, and do not wash the garment — wool will felt. A professional will use amyl acetate and NSD under controlled cold conditions.

steps: 1. Fiber test: Apply amyl acetate to a hidden seam for 30 seconds. Check for scale damage, hand change, or color bleed. If any occurs, stop — refer to wool damage protocol.

2. Apply amyl acetate to the polish film with a clean white cloth, cold and static — no scrubbing, no agitation. Tight blotting motion in concentric passes from stain edge inward. 5-10 min total dwell, refreshing the cloth each pass.
3. Apply NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) sparingly with minimum moisture. Blot dry between passes. 3-5 min. Never immerse wool.
4. Apply acetic acid 28% diluted 1:10 via dropper for pH restoration. 1-2 min. Blot. Acidic finish closes wool scales.
5. Air dry flat on absorbent padding. Do NOT use heat, do NOT hang wet, do NOT tumble. Re-inspect for hand loss or felting before returning to customer.
warnings: NEVER use acetone on wool — causes permanent hand loss and scale crazing
NEVER use nail polish remover (acetone OR non-acetone) on wool — uncontrolled ester concentrations damage keratin
NEVER use isopropyl alcohol — strips wool dyes and raises scales
NEVER agitate wet wool — felts permanently
NEVER apply heat during or after spotting — contributes to felting
Always fiber-test amyl acetate on a hidden seam

neverDo.reason: Dehydrates wool keratin, causes permanent hand loss and scale crazing.
Both formulations contain ester solvents in uncontrolled concentrations. Damage wool keratin.
Strips wool dyes and raises cuticle scales. Permanent color and hand loss.
Felts wool permanently. Any friction combined with moisture causes irreversible shrinkage and hand change.
Contributes to felting and sets nitrocellulose film. Wool spotting must be cold.

safetyMatrix.customerAdvice: Do not attempt home removal. Do not rub, do not apply ANY liquid, do not wash. Take to a professional dry cleaner with wool experience. Tell them the fiber is wool and the stain is nail polish.

escalation.whenToEscalate: Immediately — this is a professional-only case.
escalation.whatToTell: Take to a dry cleaner experienced with wool. Tell them: nail polish on wool, no home treatment attempted, no water applied, not rubbed. Do not use acetone, do not use 'non-acetone' polish remover, do not wash. Professional cold amyl acetate spotting is the only safe route.

timeEstimate: Professional only — do not attempt at home
aftercare: If the polish is still wet, blot the excess gently from the outside of the stain inward with a dry white cloth. Do NOT rub — friction felts wool. Do NOT press deeper. Do NOT apply any liquid — water plus friction is how wool felts, and the damage is permanent. If the polish has already dried, skip to step 2.

homeSolutions.agent: This is the one stain we tell you NOT to treat at home
homeSolutions.instruction: Nail polish on wool is the rare case where the best home move is no move. Every solvent that dissolves nail polish (acetone, non-acetone nail polish remover, rubbing alcohol) destroys wool. Every mechanical attempt (scrubbing, scraping wet polish) felts the fiber permanently. And water alone does nothing — nitrocellulose lacquer is not water-soluble. Don't experiment. Don't reach for "just a little" of anything. The path to saving the garment is getting it to a pro BEFORE you've applied a single thing.

homeSolutions.agent: If the polish is still wet, blot — that's all
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab gently with a dry white cloth or paper towel, from the OUTSIDE of the stain inward. Lift straight up — do NOT wipe, do NOT press deeper, do NOT apply water or any liquid. You're just picking up surface polish before it sets. Two or three dabs, then stop. Any more and you're spreading or pressing polish into the weave.

homeSolutions.agent: If the polish has already dried, leave it alone
homeSolutions.instruction: A hardened nail polish deposit sitting on wool is easier for a pro to remove than one that's been worked on. Do NOT scrape with a fingernail, a knife edge, or a card — scraping pulls wool fibers. Do NOT pick at it. Do NOT attempt to soften it with heat or water or anything. Just get the garment to a dry cleaner.

homeSolutions.agent: Go straight to a professional — ideally within 24 hours
homeSolutions.instruction: Take the garment to a dry cleaner who handles wool and delicates (confirm by phone before you go — not every cleaner can treat this). Tell them: "Nail polish on wool — [wet still / dried]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing." That specific handoff is critical — if you tell them what you DIDN'T do, they can use amyl acetate or a controlled-dwell dry-side solvent safely. If you've already applied something, tell them exactly what and for how long.
